coredata.dc:title: Multilayer pH-responsive hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles with charge reversal for drug delivery and real-time monitoring by fluorescence
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Shu
Teng, Yi
Qu, Zhengyang
Bai, Lu
Yang, Wenxiao
Wu, Qiuhua
Zhang, Guolin
coredata.dc:description: Drug delivery systems (DDSs) with charge reversal could simultaneously achieve long circulation time and enhanced cellular internalization efficiency. Here, hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (HMSNs) were used as the basic stuff for encapsulating doxorubicin (DOX), positive-charged chitosan (CS) was grafted on the surface of HMSNs, and negatively charged carboxyl-rich carbon dots (CDs) were coated on the outer layer of CS through non-covalent bonds. The new drug delivery vehicles with 56.6 % drug-loading content were prepared, named as CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX. Tumor environment with a slightly acidic state could trigger the disruption of the non-covalent bonds, followed by the detachment of CDs, making the negatively charged CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX change into positively charged CS/HMSNs@DOX. CS swelling reaction also happened in slightly acidic conditions. Both the CS swelling and the detachment of CDs resulted in the exposure of mesopore and drug release. Fluorescence recovery of detached CDs could be used to monitor the release of DOX in real-time. The multilayer pH-responsive nano carries with charge reversal and real-time monitoring demonstrated broad application prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Charge reversal
Drug delivery
Hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles
Real-time monitor
PH-responsive

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Drug loading and release of CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Start-up Funds for Doctoral Research of Liaoning University
Natural Science Foundation for Education Department of Liaoning Province of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation for Education Department of Liaoning Province of China (No. LJC201906) and Start-up funds for doctoral research of Liaoning University (a280008030).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthetic route and the schematic diagram of working mechanism of CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX.
SEM image of sSiO2 (A), TEM image of HMSNs (B), SEM (C) and TEM (D) images of CDs-CS/HMSNs.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (A), pore size distribution (B) and enlarged image of pore size distribution (C) of HMSNs, CS/HMSNs and CDs-CS/HMSNs.
DOX release graph of CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX in different PBS (T = 37 °C).

Emission spectra of CDs-CS/HMSNs in supernatant at different pH, pH = 5.0 (A), pH = 6.5 (B), pH = 7.4 (C); Relationship between fluorescence intensity and time at different pH, pH = 5.0 (D), pH = 6.5 (E), pH = 7.4 (F).

In vitro cytotoxicity of HMSNs@DOX, free DOX and CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX after incubating for 24 h with A549 cells (A) and HeLa cells (B); Fluorescence images of calcein-AM (green)/PI (red) co-staining cells (HeLa) with PBS (a), HMSNs@DOX (b) and CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX (c) (C). Data were given as means ± SD (n = 3), where *p < 0.05; ** p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001 were supposed statistically significant.
Inverted fluorescence microscopy images of HeLa cells after treatments with drug-loaded nanoparticles for 4 h (A) and 8 h (B) (blue: DAPI; red: DOX; green: CDs).

head.ce:title: Multilayer pH-responsive hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles with charge reversal for drug delivery and real-time monitoring by fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University), College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University), College of Chemistry, Liaoning University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University), College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Drug delivery systems (DDSs) with charge reversal could simultaneously achieve long circulation time and enhanced cellular internalization efficiency. Here, hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (HMSNs) were used as the basic stuff for encapsulating doxorubicin (DOX), positive-charged chitosan (CS) was grafted on the surface of HMSNs, and negatively charged carboxyl-rich carbon dots (CDs) were coated on the outer layer of CS through non-covalent bonds. The new drug delivery vehicles with 56.6 % drug-loading content were prepared, named as CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX. Tumor environment with a slightly acidic state could trigger the disruption of the non-covalent bonds, followed by the detachment of CDs, making the negatively charged CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX change into positively charged CS/HMSNs@DOX. CS swelling reaction also happened in slightly acidic conditions. Both the CS swelling and the detachment of CDs resulted in the exposure of mesopore and drug release. Fluorescence recovery of detached CDs could be used to monitor the release of DOX in real-time. The multilayer pH-responsive nano carries with charge reversal and real-time monitoring demonstrated broad application prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, many advanced drug delivery systems (DDSs) are used widely to weaken the adverse effects of anticancer drugs during chemotherapy [1–5]. Among various DDSs, hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (HMSNs) with excellent biocompatibility, large drug loading cavity and conveniency for surface functionalization have been widely adopted as substrate materials. However, bare HMSNs cannot prevent drug leakage during blood circulation and are easily cleared by macrophages. Therefore, it is necessary to connect capping agents on the outer layer of HMSNs as gatekeepers to prepare drug carriers with different structures and properties. The gatekeepers of HMSNs can be, for example, polysaccharides, polymers, dendrimers, etc. [6–12] and could endow carriers with responsiveness. The pH in the microenvironment (pH < 6.5) and endosomes/lysosomes of tumor (pH = 4.0–6.0) are different from that in normal tissue (pH = 7.3–7.4). Many pH-responsive DDSs have been designed according to the difference to realize intelligent release.

With good biocompatibility, non-toxicity and biodegradability, chitosan (CS) rich in amino groups is a natural polysaccharide that can be selected as the gatekeeper of HMSNs to prevent drug leakage [13]. pH-responsive CS layer will undergo swelling reaction due to electrostatic repulsion and solvation under acidic conditions, so it can realize pH-triggered drug release. However, the nanoparticles are positively charged in an acidic and neutral atmosphere due to the surface of CS being filled with amino groups. Positively charged nanoparticles have strong affinity with cell membranes and a fine targeting effect. They also have strong interaction with serum components and are easily cleared by macrophages. Thus, the possibility of reaching the tumor site is reduced and the side effects were increased. Moreover, the poor water solubility of CS also results in the increase of side effect. Connecting negatively charged gatekeepers to the outer layer of CS could not only prolong their circulation time in blood, but also prevent early drug leakage and improve water solubility.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are very promising nanomaterials with good biocompatibility, high water solubility, low toxicity, low cost, easy surface modification, and particle size less than 10 nm [14–22]. There are a large number of functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as amino, hydroxyl or carboxyl groups, while CS was rich in amino and hydroxyl groups. CDs were negatively charged, while CS was positively charged. Thus, CDs could be easily grafted onto the outer layer of CS as a "protective layer" through hydrogen bonds or electrostatic interactions. CDs can not only make the particles negatively charged to escape the clearance by macrophages, but also endow the particles with excellent water dispersibility.

New DDSs (CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX) with fine biodegradability, biocompatibility and long circulation time were designed in this work. The drug releasing and loading characteristics were evaluated via doxorubicin (DOX). CS was grafted on the surface of HMSNs@DOX through crosslinking agent glycidoxypropyl-trimethoxy-silane (GPTMS) [9]. CDs could be anchored outside of CS as a protective layer to prolong the circulation time of CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX in blood via non-covalent bonding (electrostatic interactions or hydrogen bonds). Under tumor environment (weak acidity), the instability of pH sensitive non-covalent bonds led to the separation of CDs. Uncovered CS was protonated, so that CS/HMSNs@DOX were ingested into cancer cells. The probability of internalization of CS/HMSNs@DOX into cancer cells was improved through electrostatic interaction after the charge changed from negative to positive [23–28]. At the same time, the swelling reaction of CS occurred under the stimulation of pH, and DOX was released from CS/HMSNs@DOX. In addition, the fluorescence of CDs recovered after fell off, which was used to monitor the release of DOX. The biodegradable and biocompatible DDSs, which integrated pH responsiveness, charge reversal, and real-time monitoring of drug release in one, should have wide application prospects. The synthesis process and working mechanism of CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX can be seen in Fig. 1.

Cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), anhydrous sodium carbonate (>99.8 %) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 99 %) were provided by Aladdin Industrial Corporation (China). Urea was procured from China National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid was purchased from Innokai Technology Co., Ltd. Chitosan (CS, deacetylation ≥95 %) and Glycidoxypropyl-trimethoxy-silane (GPTMS, 97 %) were acquired from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), phosphate-buffered solution (PBS: 0.01 mol L−1) and other biological reagents were procured from Accela ChemBio Co. Ltd (China). HeLa cervical cancer cells, Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Penicillin-Streptomycin (P/S) Solution, Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) and Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 were purchased from Wuhan punuosai Life Technology Co., Ltd. A549 lung cancer cells were procured from Shanghai Gaining Biotechnology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received.

CDs rich in carboxyl groups were synthesized based on the method in previous literature [29]. A suspension containing citric acid (3 g), urea (6 g) and ultrapure water (10 mL) was prepared. Then, the suspension was reacted for 4–5 min using microwave radiation method (Midea Microwave M1-L202B, Foshan, China). The obtained brown powder was transferred in a vacuum oven and maintained at 60 °C for 1 h to obtain CDs. Ultrapure water (10 mL) was used to disperse the prepared CDs and then centrifuged to remove macromolecular clusters. The refined CDs were lyophilized.

The HMSNs were prepared by etching method [30] and the relevant specifics were in the supporting information. The solution of acetic acid (100 μL) and ultrapure water (10 mL) was prepared, and then CS (100 mg) was uniformly dissolved in it to obtain CS solution. GPTMS (500 μL) was added to ethanol (10 mL) to prepare the GPTMS ethanol solution. CS solution (5 mL) and GPTMS ethanol solution (200 μL) were mixed, and then the mixture was diluted to 20 mL by ethanol. The blend was transferred to a closed container and stirred for 24 h at 25 °C. HMSNs (128 mg) were ultrasonically dispersed in the reaction mixture and stirred at 25 °C for 12 h. After centrifugation, ultrapure water and ethanol were employed to wash the precipitate 3 times respectively. CS/HMSNs were obtained by freeze-drying finally.

CDs (30 mg) and CS/HMSNs (50 mg) were then dispersed into PBS (pH = 7.4, 50 mL) and maintained at 25 °C for 12 h under stirring. Powder was gotten by centrifugation and washed with PBS (pH = 7.4) 3 times. CDs-CS/HMSNs were obtained by freeze-drying.

CDs-CS/HMSNs (50 mg) were dispersed in PBS (pH = 5.0, 50 mL, at 37 °C) under uniform stirring. The sample solution (1 mL) was taken out at a specific time for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation to evaluate their degradability.

Dispersion of CDs-CS/HMSNs (1 mg mL−1) was prepared using PBS with different pH values (pH = 7.4 and 5.0) as diluent at 25 °C under uniform stirring. After soaking in PBS for 30 min, the sample solution (1 mL) was taken out. The swelling effect was characterized by TEM and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS).

A series of CDs aqueous solutions (200 µg mL−1) were formulated with pH 1–14. The fluorescence emission spectra excited at 440 nm were acquired at different pH (temperature). A curve of I/I0 against pH (temperature) was plotted to assess the steadiness of CDs, where I and I0 represented the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm under different pH (temperature) and neutral pH (25 °C), respectively.

HMSNs (50 mg) were added to the PBS solution of DOX (1.0 mg mL−1, 20 mL, pH = 7.4), and stirred in darkness for 48 h. After centrifugation and gently washing with PBS three times, HMSNs@DOX were obtained by freeze-drying. The supernatant and all the washing residuals were mixed and the absorbance at 485 nm was measured to quantify the DOX remained in solution (unloaded into HMSNs). The details about the preparation of DOX absorbance-concentration curve could be found in the supporting information. The amount of unloaded DOX could be obtained through the standard equation, thereby obtaining the amount of "total loaded DOX" (subtracting the amount unloaded into HMSNs from the total amount of DOX added).

Then, HMSNs@DOX were coated with CS using GPTMS as a cross-linker, followed by connecting the protective layer CDs (CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX). Drug loading content (DLC) was obtained as following:
DLC
( % ) =
total loaded DOX
total sample + total loaded DOX
× 100 %

The drug release properties in presence of different PBS were investigated. CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX (4 mg) were dispersed in a buffer solution (4 mL) and dialyzed (MWCO: 3k da) in the buffer solution (20 mL) for 32 h. A certain amount of dialysate (4 mL) was taken out regularly and equivalent new buffer solution was replenished. The absorbance of the solution was analyzed by a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 485 nm.

CDs-CS/HMSNs (3 mg) were spread out in PBS with different pH values (50 mL) and stirred at 37 °C. At regular intervals, the extracted suspension (3 mL) was centrifuged and the supernatant was recovered. The fluorescence of the supernatant was recorded.

A549 lung cancer cells and HeLa cervical cancer cells were respectively from the Chinese Academy of Sciences and a domestic key laboratory, HEK293T human embryonic kidney cells were derived from Fudan cell bank. The cells were incubated in RPMI-1640 (A549) and DMEM (HeLa and HEK293T) containing 1 % P/S and 10 % FBS. The cell culture environment was in a humidified incubator filled at 37 °C with 5 % CO2.

A549 cells and HeLa cells were culture with free DOX, HMSNs, CDs, CDs-CS/HMSNs, HMSNs-DOX and CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX to investigated their cytotoxicities. Meanwhile, the cytotoxicities of HMSNs, CDs and CDs-CS/HMSNs were also evaluated using normal cells (HEK293T cells). Cells were added to a 96-well dish with fresh medium (100 μL) at a density of 105 cells/well and incubated for 48 h. Then, fresh medium (100 μL, containing a series of nanoparticles with different concentrations) replaced the old medium. After 24 h, each well was filled with MTT solution (20 μL, 5.0 mg mL−1), and the cultivation was continued for an extra 4 h. At last, the generated MTT formazan crystals were dissolved by DMSO (150 μL), and the optical density (OD) of each well was detected at 492 nm via a microplate reader. The cell viability (%) was acquired using the formula:
C ell viability
( % ) = (
OD sample
− OD blank
OD control
− OD blank
) × 100 %
All tests were replicated at least 3 times.

HeLa cells were seeded in a 24-well plate with the density of 1 × 105 and cultured in an incubator for 48 h. Then the old medium was sucked away and the fresh medium containing HMSNs@DOX and CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX (holding 50 µg mL−1 DOX) was added. After 4 h, the cells were co incubated with calcein-AM/PI solution for staining purposes according to the product description. Using PBS as a negative control. Finally, an inverted fluorescence microscope was used to analyze the results (green represented living cells, while red represented dead cells).

A549 cells or HeLa cells were seeded in a 12-well dish with the density of 105 cells/well and cultured in an incubator for 48 h. Then the fresh medium containing different nanoparticles (holding 50 µg mL−1 DOX) took the place of old medium. After dosing treatments for a period of time, the cells were stained with DAPI (20 µL) at 37 °C for 10 min, followed by washing each hole with PBS to remove the excess DAPI. Finally, the cells were observed by an inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon Ts2-FL).
Data were given as mean ± SD. Significant differences between the two means were analyzed via one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, and ***P < 0.001.

The morphology, size distribution, structure and zeta potential of nanoparticles were determined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi H-7650, 100 KV), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOLJEM-2100, 200 kV) and Malvern Nano ZS, respectively. The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of nanoparticles were collected in transmission from KBr pellets on an FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet 5700). The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the products under a nitrogen atmosphere were recorded by an SDT Q600 simultaneous DSC/DTA-TGA instrument. The fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu RF-5301PC) was used to investigate the fluorescence properties. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was employed to determine the specific surface area and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model was employed to determine the pore size distribution.

CDs were prepared by microwave assistance (Scheme S1). TEM image (Fig. S2A) suggested that CDs were spherical with fine dispersibility and particle sizes of 2–6 nm, which was further verified by DLS (Fig. S2B). Fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. S2C) indicated that the CDs solutions displayed the strongest emission intensity at 520 nm (excited at 440 nm). The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S2D) displayed that the wide absorption band at 3053–3556 cm−1 belonged to N-H and O-H, and the absorption band at 1600–1726 cm−1 belonged to CN and CO. These functional groups enhanced the hydrophilicity and stability of CDs. According to Fig. S3, I/I0 did not show significant fluctuations within a certain pH (4−12) range and temperature (4–70 °C), demonstrating the steadiness of the fluorescence properties of CDs.

sSiO2 nanoparticles were prepared first as sacrificial templates and the SEM image ( Fig. 2A) demonstrated that the obtained nanoparticles were smooth and well-dispersed spheres (sSiO2) with a mean diameter of 110 nm.

Coating silica shell and subsequent etching gave HMSNs. As shown in Fig. 2B, HMSNs exhibited large cavity structures and well-dispersed spheres with a particle size of 178 nm, which was also characterized through DLS measurement (Fig. S4). FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S5A) also certified the successful preparation of HMSNs [31–33]. All samples exhibited characteristic absorption peaks at approximately 1090, 805, and 465 cm−1, representing the asymmetric stretching, symmetric stretching, and bending vibrations of Si-O-Si, respectively. The vibration of Si-OH belonging to HMSNs could be observed at 960 cm−1. The high number of Si-OH groups resulted in a negative potential of HMSNs (Fig. S5B). It could be seen from Fig. S5C that bare HMSNs only have a 12.1 % mass loss, which could be attributed to solvent molecules, humidity, CTAB, etc [34–37]. Fig. 3A and B demonstrated that the synthesized HMSNs exhibited a typical IV-type curve, with a high specific surface area of 772.4 m2 g−1 and a pore volume of 0.68 cc g−1.

Anchoring CS onto the surface of HMSNs was carried out using crosslinking agent GPTMS. The FT-IR spectrum of CS/HMSNs was recorded (Fig. S5A). The peaks at 1573 cm−1 and 1658 cm−1 were assigned to NH2 on CS. Zeta potential (Fig. S5B) became positive. Another 7.0 % mass loss in TGA graph (Fig. S5C) of CS/HMSNs compared to HMSNs was attributed to CS and GPTMS. Fig. 3A and B indicated that, the specific surface area and pore volume of CS/HMSNs were 121.6 m2 g−1 and 0.23 cc g−1, respectively.

As the molar ratio of urea to citric acid was 2:1, the obtained CDs exhibited rich carboxyl groups on their surface [38]. CDs were negatively charged, while CS was positively charged. The electrostatic interactions between them resulted the formation of CDs-CS/HMSNs. There are many the amino and hydroxyl groups on CS, which could also form a hydrogen-bonding interaction with the hydroxyl, amino or carboxylic acid groups on CDs. Compared to electrostatic interactions, hydrogen-bonding played a secondary role [39,40]. The surface of CDs-CS/HMSNs (Fig. 2C) was rough and their diameter (Fig. 2D) increased to 190 nm. Compared with the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S5A) of CS/HMSNs, the amino peaks at 1573 cm−1 and 1658 cm−1 of CDs-CS/HMSNs showed a certain degree of weakening. A weak CO peak at 1715 cm−1 which was absent in the FT-IR of CS/HMSNs appeared, indicating that carboxyl-rich CDs were grafted onto the outer layer of CS/HMSNs. After depositing negatively charged CDs, the zeta potential (Fig. S5B) became negative. Fig. S5C showed that CDs-CS/HMSNs generated a weight loss of another 5.8 % compared to CS/HMSNs. Fig. 3A, B and C demonstrated that the specific surface area and pore volume of CDs-CS/HMSNs decreased from 121.6 m2 g−1 and 0.23 cc g−1 to 24.3 m2 g−1 and 0.14 cc g−1, respectively, and the pore size of CDs-CS/HMSNs also decreased after grafting.

The degradability of CDs-CS/HMSNs was studied in Fig. S6. The results showed that the degradation rate of CDs-CS/HMSNs approached 30 % on the 5th day and approached 80 % on the 7th day. After the detachment of the CDs and complete dissipation of CS layer, the subsequent degradation of HMSNs could be attributed to their thin pore walls and large surface area. DDSs with fine degradability could promote the complete release of drugs and reduce the side effects caused by the retention of materials in human body.

The pH sensitivity of the CDs-CS/HMSNs originated from the pH sensitive non-covalent bonds and pH-dependent swelling behavior of CS layer. The Zeta potential value of CDs-CS/HMSNs at different pH values were measured (Fig. S7). The Zeta potential of CDs-CS/HMSNs was significantly higher (17.6) at acidic PBS than that at neutral PBS (-7.7). The difference was huge and charge reversal could be achieved when the pH value changed from neutral to weakly acidic. This was attributed not only to the protonation of the functional groups on the surface of CS/HMSNs, but also to the non-covalent bonds. The disruption of the non-covalent bonds in slightly acidic PBS resulted in the separation of negatively charged CDs and the exposure of positively charged CS, leading to the significantly raising of Zeta potential value.

The TEM images of CDs-CS/HMSNs after soaking in neutral PBS (pH = 7.4, Fig. S8A) and in acidic PBS (pH = 5.0, Fig. S8B) were showed. The morphology and average diameter of the particles soaked in neutral PBS (pH = 7.4) were similar to those before soaking (Fig. 2D). The morphology of particles soaked in acidic PBS (pH = 5.0) was also sphere, while the average diameter increased from 190 nm to 240 nm. The change in average diameter was mainly attributed to the swelling of the chitosan part (approximately 6 nm to 31 nm), while the internal structure of HMSNs remains unchanged (Fig. S8B). DLS results (Fig. S8C) were corresponding to those of TEM. Under acidic conditions, the non-covalent bonds were disrupted, leading to the separation of capping agent CDs. Then, electrostatic repulsion and solvation effect of the exposed CS layer resulted in the swelling reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and release of CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the large cavity and mesoporous shell structure of HMSNs, DOX could be effectively loaded. Then, the CS layer was grafted onto the surface of HMSNs through crosslinking agent GPTMS to prevent premature leakage of DOX, followed by CDs modification. The synthetic route of CDs-CS/HMSN@DOX was shown in Scheme S3. The drug loading content of CDs-CS/HMSN@DOX was 56.6 %.

CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX were dispersed in different media to evaluate the DOX release behaviors ( Fig. 4). The cumulative release amount of DOX within 32 h at 37 °C gradually increased with the decrease of pH value. The cumulative release amount within 32 h was 65.39 % (at pH 5.0), which was 3.27 times and 6.19 times higher than that at pH 6.5 (20.02 %) and pH 7.4 (10.56 %), respectively. The pH-dependent drug release property was attributed to dual pH-responsive of CDs-CS/HMSNs (namely the sensitivity of the non-covalent bonding to pH and the swelling reaction of the CS layer under acidic conditions). This also indicated that CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX have controlled release behavior.

The CDs exhibited strong fluorescence emission, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs-CS/HMSNs (Fig. S9) significantly decreased and was almost quenched. Those might be attributed to the difference in surface state and the infamous phenomenon of aggregation induced luminescence quenching [41–43]. CDs were grafted onto the outer layer of CS/HMSNs through non-covalent bonds, resulting the changing of surface state.

The fluorescence properties of CDs-CS/HMSNs in PBS with different pH were characterized ( Fig. 5). The intensity increased with time and the increasing speeds were different at different pH. It was faster at lower pH and lower at higher pH. The results were attributed to non-covalent bonds. These bonds were sensitive to environmental pH, meaning breaking under mild acidity, e.g. pH = 5.0 and binding under neutral pH [29]. CDs were detached under acidic conditions. Their sizes and the surface state were the same as those of CDs before grafting, and their fluorescence were recovered.

For drug-loaded carriers, the disruption of non-covalent bonds under acidic conditions and the separation of CDs implied the release of cargo. The fluorescence recovery and the drug release should be synchronous. Therefore, the cumulative release of DOX and the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 520 nm for emission peak (pH = 5.0) were fitted, and a good linear relationship was found (Fig. S10), indicating that the drug release could be monitored in real-time through fluorescence intensity.

Both MTT and calcein-AM/PI co-staining assay were employed to analyze the influence of nanoparticles on cell survival in vitro. Two kinds of tumor cells were employed in MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S11, after incubation with HMSNs, CDs, and CDs-CS/HMSNs for 24 h, there was no significant cell apoptosis, indicating the blank carrier without DOX was non-toxic to A549 cells and HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. S12, HMSNs, CDs, and CDs-CS/HMSNs were incubated with normal cells (HEK293T cells), and after 48 h of incubation, the cell viability of HEK293T cells was more than 90 %, which indicated that the nanoparticles showed good biocompatibility and had low toxicity to normal cells. However, it could be seen from Fig. 6A and B that drug-loaded particles (HMSNs@DOX, free DOX and CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX) could reduce cell activity, and their cytotoxicities came from DOX (free DOX or released DOX). After incubation with A549 cells and HeLa cells for 24 h, the cytotoxicity of free DOX was higher than that of HMSNs@DOX. This was attributed to the encapsulation of HMSNs hindering the full effect of DOX on cancer cells. The cytotoxicity of CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX was larger than those of HMSNs@DOX and free DOX. The results were due to the disruption of the non-covalent bonds under acidic conditions and charge reversal. Negatively charged CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX transformed to positively charged CS/HMSNs@DOX, which was inclined to be absorbed by negatively charged cell membranes and provided with enhanced internalization efficiency. Nano carries with charge reversal possessed fine targeting efficiency.

As shown in Fig. 6C, calcein-AM/PI co-staining assay gave almost the same results as MTT assay. The cells incubated with CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX showed very strong red fluorescence, indicating more cancer cell apoptosis.

The endocytosis and intracellular release characters of HMSNs@DOX and CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX in HeLa cells ( Fig. 7) and A549 cells (Fig. S13) were evaluated by cell uptake experiment. After co-culture of nanoparticles and cells for 4 h, obvious red fluorescence representing DOX and green fluorescence representing CDs were observed and they overlapped with blue fluorescence representing the nucleus. The results indicated that the nanoparticles reached the nucleus and the acidic intracellular surrounding triggered separation of CDs and release of DOX. The fluorescence intensities increased with the cultivation time, implying that more DOX was released and acted on the nucleus to inhibit the proliferation of cancer cells. However, the fluorescence intensity in cells treated with HMSNs@DOX was lower than those incubated with CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX. The results were equivalent to the results of cytotoxicity, and also indicated that the CDs-CS/HMSNs@DOX realized charge reverse and possess enhanced internalization efficiency.

In this work, new multilayer pH-responsive degradable nano vehicles based on HMSNs (CDs-CS/HMSNs) were successfully prepared for drug delivery. This novel DDSs showed good biocompatibility, biodegradability, high drug loading, the controllable release behavior and could achieve real-time monitoring of drug release through the shedding of CDs. Meanwhile, the charge reversal caused by acidic conditions could increase cellular uptake and promote apoptosis of cancer cells. This work may open up new research avenues for charge reverse transfer drug systems.

Lu Bai: Supervision, Methodology. Zhengyang Qu: Methodology, Conceptualization. Qiuhua Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. Wenxiao Yang: Supervision, Formal analysis. Yi Teng: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shu Wu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Guolin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation for Education Department of Liaoning Province of China (No. LJC201906) and Start-up Funds for Doctoral Research of Liaoning University (a280008030).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.133831.
